title: Introduction to Variables
description: Learn what variables are and how to declare them using var, let, and const.
longDescription: Variables are fundamental to programming. They allow you to store values, give them meaningful names, and reuse them throughout your code. In JavaScript, you have three options for declaring variables: var, let, and const. Each has its own use cases and rules.

learningObjectives.statement: Understand what a variable is and why we use them
Declare variables using let and const
Explain the difference between let and const

missionNarrative.title: The Variable Vault
missionNarrative.story: Deep in the Code Caverns lies the Variable Vault, where ancient programmers stored their most precious data. To enter, you must learn the sacred incantations: let, const, and the forgotten var...

sections.title: What Are Variables?
sections.content:
Imagine you're packing for a trip. You wouldn't just throw everything into one big pile, right? You'd use labeled boxes - one for clothes, one for toiletries, one for electronics.

**Variables work the same way!** They're labeled containers that hold information in your program.

sections.title: Declaring Variables
Pro Tip
sections.content: **Always prefer const** unless you know the value will change. This makes your code more predictable and easier to debug!

sections.title: Variable Naming Rules
sections.content:
## Rules for Naming Variables

1. **Must start with** a letter, underscore (_), or dollar sign ($)
2. **Can contain** letters, numbers, underscores, and dollar signs
3. **Are case-sensitive** (playerName ≠ PlayerName)
4. **Cannot use** reserved keywords (if, for, while, etc.)

## Best Practices

- Use **camelCase** for multi-word names
- Choose **descriptive names** over abbreviations
- Be **consistent** with your naming style

sections.title: Good vs Bad Variable Names

challenges.title: Identify Valid Variable Names
challenges.description: Test your understanding of variable naming rules
challenges.question: Which of these are valid JavaScript variable names?
challenges.instructions: Select ALL valid variable names from the options below.

options.explanation: Invalid! Variable names cannot start with a number.
Valid! Underscores are allowed at the start.
Invalid! 'class' is a reserved keyword in JavaScript.
Valid! Dollar signs are allowed (often used for DOM elements).

challenges.successFeedback: Excellent! You've mastered variable naming rules!
challenges.failureFeedback: Not quite. Review the rules: names can't start with numbers and can't use reserved keywords.

hints.text: Think about what characters are allowed at the start of a variable name.
Some words have special meaning in JavaScript and can't be used as variable names.
Numbers can't be first, but symbols like _ and $ can!

hints.finalExplanation: Variable names must start with a letter, underscore, or dollar sign. They cannot be reserved keywords like 'class', 'function', 'if', etc.

challenges.title: Choose the Right Declaration
challenges.description: Decide whether to use let or const for each scenario
challenges.question: Complete the code with 'let' or 'const'
challenges.instructions: Fill in each blank with either 'let' or 'const' based on whether the value should change.
challenges.successFeedback: Perfect! You understand when to use let vs const!
challenges.failureFeedback: Remember: use const for values that won't change, let for values that will.

hints.text: Ask yourself: Will this value need to change later?
Constants (unchanging values) should use const. Variables (changing values) should use let.

hints.finalExplanation: PI and TAX_RATE are constants - they never change, so use const. clickCount and userName will change, so use let.

reflectionQuestion.question: In your own words, explain when you would use 'let' versus 'const'. Give an example from your own experience or imagine a scenario.
reflectionQuestion.sampleAnswer: I would use 'const' for values that should never change, like mathematical constants (PI), configuration values, or fixed settings. I would use 'let' for values that need to change, like counters in loops, user input that can be updated, or temporary calculations.
